meta_title: Why Do Windows Get Dirty So Fast? Shuswap Causes & Fixes
meta_desc: If your windows re-dirty within weeks of cleaning, there are specific causes to investigate. A Shuswap-specific guide to sprinklers, cottonwood, lake spray, and more.
h1: Why Do My Windows Get Dirty So Fast? Causes and Solutions for Shuswap Homes
intro: If you're getting your windows cleaned and they look grimy again within a few weeks, you're not imagining it — and it's not that the cleaning was poor. Specific environmental factors in the Shuswap region cause windows to re-dirty faster than the national average, and each one has a different solution. Here's what to look for and what to do about it.

sections.h2: Sprinkler Overspray: The Most Common Culprit
sections.body:
Irrigation sprinkler overspray is the single most common cause of rapid window re-dirtying in BC residential properties, and it's almost always fixable without spending more on cleaning.

Here's the problem: residential irrigation water, whether municipal or well source, contains dissolved minerals — primarily calcium and magnesium. When sprinkler heads spray water that contacts window glass, the water evaporates and leaves the mineral content behind as a white, chalky residue. Unlike normal rain (which is relatively mineral-free and flows off), sprinkler water deposits minerals in the same spot repeatedly, building up layers of calcium carbonate that become progressively harder to remove.

In Salmon Arm and the surrounding Shuswap region, the municipal water supply has moderate mineral hardness. Well water on rural properties near the benchlands above town can be significantly harder. If your sprinklers are hitting your windows even once a week, you're depositing minerals on that glass 26+ times per year. No cleaning schedule addresses this if the source problem isn't fixed.

The fix is straightforward in most cases: adjust your sprinkler heads to direct spray away from windows. Most residential rotary and impact heads have an adjustment screw for arc and distance. A landscape or irrigation company can adjust heads professionally in an hour. Alternatively, adding a deflector shield on heads that are positioned to spray toward the house is a quick DIY fix.

For cases where head adjustment isn't possible — irrigation systems with fixed-arc heads or heads positioned at awkward angles — the practical solution may be adjusting the watering schedule so sprinklers run in the early morning when the sun is low and glass is cool, reducing the evaporation rate that accelerates mineral deposition.
bullets: Municipal water hardness in Salmon Arm is moderate; well water can be significantly harder
26+ mineral deposits per year from weekly sprinkler contact — no cleaning schedule beats this
Fix: adjust sprinkler head arc and distance to eliminate window contact
Alternative: early morning watering reduces evaporation rate on glass

sections.h2: Cottonwood Season in the Shuswap
sections.body:
Cottonwood is a regional phenomenon that Shuswap homeowners know well — and it's one of the worst periods for window cleanliness in the entire year. Black cottonwood (Populus trichocarpa) is native to BC's river corridors and grows abundantly in the Salmon Arm lowlands, along Shuswap Lake shores, and throughout the valley bottoms of the region. In late May to early June, cottonwood trees release massive quantities of fluffy white seed fibers into the air — a phenomenon that can last two to four weeks and turns the air visibly white in areas with dense tree populations.

Cottonwood fluff itself is large enough to see and blow off window glass, but the seed fibers carry a sticky substance that adheres to glass and frame surfaces. This adhesive residue traps fine dust and other particulate, creating a grime layer that builds faster than any other seasonal period. Windows cleaned immediately before cottonwood season will need another clean shortly after it passes.

The timing recommendation for Shuswap homes near cottonwood trees: schedule your spring clean for April — before cottonwood season — to benefit from clean glass through May. Then schedule a follow-up clean in mid-to-late June after cottonwood has finished, to clear the accumulated residue. This two-visit spring sequence accounts for the regional cottonwood factor rather than fighting it.

For homes in areas with very heavy cottonwood exposure — particularly near the Salmon River floodplain, Shuswap Lake shores, or the lower Shuswap River corridor — the follow-up clean is essentially mandatory, not optional. The residue buildup during a heavy cottonwood year can be significant.
bullets: Cottonwood season: late May to mid-June in the Shuswap corridor
Cottonwood fibers carry sticky adhesive residue that accelerates dust accumulation on glass
Timing strategy: clean in April (before), clean again in mid-June (after)
Heavy cottonwood areas near Salmon River and lake shores may need the follow-up clean every year

sections.h2: South-Facing Windows and Sun-Baked Residue
sections.body:
South-facing windows in the Shuswap receive intense UV exposure and heat loading for most of the year — and this has a direct effect on how quickly windows re-dirty and how easy they are to clean.

When cleaning solution, rain, or airborne deposit contacts warm glass in direct sun, it dries almost instantaneously. Fast drying means less time for solution to flow off the glass, so whatever dissolved minerals or particulate it contains are deposited exactly where it dried rather than running to the sill. South-facing windows in sunny climates are consistently harder to keep clean than north or east-facing windows for this reason.

The practical implication for Shuswap homeowners is that south-facing windows may need more frequent cleaning than other windows on the same house. The dirtying mechanism is photochemical as well as depositional: UV exposure accelerates the bonding of organic particulate (pollen, biological matter, traffic exhaust film) to the glass surface.

One effective mitigation is a hydrophobic glass treatment applied after a thorough professional clean. Products like Rain-X or nano-coating treatments create a water-shedding surface on the glass that causes water to bead and run off rather than spreading and drying. On south-facing glass, this significantly reduces mineral deposit accumulation and extends the clean appearance between visits. The treatment needs reapplication every 4-6 months, or after each professional clean if the cleaner's process strips it.

Timing professional cleans for south-facing windows in the early morning — when the glass is cool and shaded — also produces better results than afternoon cleans when the glass is heated by sun.
sections.body2: If you have a mix of window orientations on your home, consider discussing differential cleaning frequency with your window cleaner — south-facing windows may warrant more visits than north-facing ones.

sections.h2: Setting Realistic Expectations for Shuswap Cleaning Frequency
sections.body:
In a region with moderate water hardness, cottonwood season, wildfire smoke season, and summer heat loading, windows re-dirty faster than the BC Interior average. Understanding realistic maintenance intervals helps homeowners set expectations and budget appropriately.

For most Salmon Arm residential properties, a semi-annual professional clean (spring and fall) is the minimum viable schedule for presentable windows year-round. This gives you clean glass entering summer (after cottonwood, before the dry dusty period) and clean glass entering winter (after wildfire smoke season, before freeze-up).

Homes with the specific factors discussed — sprinkler overspray, heavy cottonwood exposure, south-facing facades — may benefit from a third clean in mid-summer (July or August). For these properties, three professional visits per year is the right baseline rather than two.

Between professional visits, a DIY touch-up with a microfiber cloth and appropriate solution on the most visible windows can maintain appearance without the cost of a full service visit. Interior glass, which re-dirties more slowly, can often be handled DIY between professional appointments.

The most cost-effective approach: address the re-dirtying sources (sprinkler adjustment, drainage improvements) before increasing clean frequency. Fixing a sprinkler that's been hitting a window for years can turn a four-visit-per-year window into a two-visit-per-year window. Source control is always cheaper than more frequent cleaning.
bullets: Semi-annual (spring and fall) is the minimum viable schedule for most Shuswap homes
Homes with sprinkler exposure, cottonwood, or south-facing glass: consider three visits per year
Fix re-dirtying sources before adding cleaning frequency — source control is cheaper
Interior glass re-dirties more slowly and can often be managed with DIY between professional visits

faqs.q: How do I know if my sprinklers are causing my window mineral deposits?
faqs.a: Check your windows after an irrigation cycle on a dry day — mineral deposits from sprinklers appear as white circular or arc-shaped patterns that match the trajectory of the spray, concentrated at the point where drops contact the glass and run down. Compare which windows have deposits with which ones are in range of irrigation heads. Turning off a specific zone for two weeks and observing whether that window's re-dirtying rate changes is a simple diagnostic.

faqs.q: Is there a glass coating that reduces how fast windows get dirty?
faqs.a: Yes. Hydrophobic surface treatments like Rain-X or nano-coating products cause water to bead and run off rather than spreading and drying on glass. This dramatically reduces mineral deposit accumulation and slows dust adhesion. The treatment lasts 3-6 months depending on UV exposure and cleaning frequency. Ask your window cleaner about applying a hydrophobic coating after your next professional clean.

faqs.q: Does wildfire smoke affect window dirtiness in the Shuswap?
faqs.a: Yes. Wildfire smoke season — typically July through September in BC interior years — deposits a fine particulate and organic compound film on all outdoor surfaces including glass. South and west-facing windows tend to accumulate more smoke particulate. A professional clean in early October, after smoke season ends and before freeze-up, is good timing for clearing this accumulation before winter.

faqs.q: My windows were just cleaned two weeks ago and they already look bad. What's happening?
faqs.a: Two weeks is very fast re-dirtying and almost always indicates an active contamination source rather than normal environmental accumulation. The most likely causes are: sprinkler overspray hitting the glass, a drain or gutter that discharges onto the window surface, or a nearby construction site generating heavy dust. Investigate which windows are dirtying fastest and what's adjacent or upwind of them.

cta_text: Windows re-dirtying faster than expected? Let's figure out why and build a cleaning schedule that actually works. Call David at (403) 837-5092.

meta_title: Can Window Cleaning Scratch Glass? Prevention & Liability
meta_desc: Window cleaning can scratch glass when done wrong. Learn how scratches happen, how professionals prevent them, what to do if scratches appear, and pre-existing damage.
h1: Can Window Cleaning Scratch Glass? How It Happens and How Professionals Prevent It
intro: Glass scratches from window cleaning are real but preventable — and when they do occur, there's a clear framework for determining whether they're the cleaner's responsibility or a pre-existing condition. Whether you're a homeowner evaluating a DIY approach or assessing a cleaning company's practices, here's what you need to know about glass and scratching risk.

sections.h2: How Glass Gets Scratched During Cleaning
sections.body:
Glass scratching during window cleaning happens through specific, understood mechanisms. Knowing them allows you to either prevent them in DIY cleaning or evaluate whether a professional company uses appropriate precautions.

**Abrasive grit under the squeegee**: The most common cause of cleaning-induced scratches is abrasive particle (sand, grit, or hardened mineral deposits) being trapped between the squeegee rubber and the glass surface, then dragged across the glass under pressure. The squeegee acts as a vehicle for the abrasive, and the pressure behind the stroke drives it into the glass surface. This is why professional cleaners pre-rinse glass with water before applying any scrubber or squeegee — the pre-rinse floats loose abrasive off the glass surface so it doesn't get dragged.

**Steel wool or abrasive scrubbers on inappropriate glass**: Steel wool (0000 grade is commonly used for stain removal) is appropriate on some glass types but dangerous on others. On standard annealed (float) glass, fine steel wool used correctly can remove mineral deposits without scratching. On tempered glass, the same steel wool can cause significant scratching due to the tempered glass surface's different properties. Identifying glass type before selecting cleaning tools is a basic professional competency.

**Razor blades used incorrectly**: A razor blade scraper is a legitimate window cleaning tool for paint overspray, hard adhesive, and bonded mineral deposits. Used at the wrong angle, with dull blades, on tempered glass without adequate lubrication, or on glass that has been scored or damaged previously, a razor blade creates permanent scratches. New blades, correct angle (less than 30 degrees to the glass surface), and adequate lubrication from cleaning solution are the three non-negotiable requirements.

**Hard-bristle brushes on tempered or coated glass**: Water-fed pole systems use brushes to agitate cleaning solution on upper-story glass. Brush bristle hardness should be matched to the glass type. Hard-bristle brushes on soft-coated or filmed glass surfaces (low-E coatings, solar control films) can scratch the coating even if they don't scratch the glass substrate.
bullets: Grit under squeegee: trapped abrasive dragged under pressure is the most common scratch mechanism
Pre-rinse protocol: floating grit off glass before any scrubber contact is the primary prevention step
Steel wool: appropriate on annealed glass, can scratch tempered glass
Razor blades: correct angle, sharp blade, and lubrication are non-negotiable
Hard brushes: inappropriate for coated, filmed, or soft-surface glass types

sections.h2: What Professional Cleaners Do to Prevent Scratches
sections.body:
A professional window cleaning company's approach to scratch prevention reveals their overall quality level. Here's what a competent company does.

**Pre-rinse before contact cleaning**: Every exterior glass surface should be rinsed with low-pressure water before any scrubber, squeegee, or blade makes contact. This is not a time-saving step that can be skipped in dry conditions — dry conditions often mean more abrasive grit on the glass. Pre-rinsing is the single most important scratch prevention measure.

**Tempered glass identification**: Tempered glass — required by BC building code on all windows within 50cm of a door, windows lower than 45cm from the floor, large window units in certain configurations, and patio sliding doors — must be identified before selecting cleaning tools. Tempered glass can often be identified by the small permanent etch mark (manufacturer code) in one corner. If the stamp is absent or unclear, treating the glass as tempered is the conservative approach.

**Fresh razor blades and correct technique**: Any professional cleaner using a razor blade on glass should be using a fresh blade for each job (blades dull quickly and are inexpensive to replace) and holding the blade at the correct angle with constant even pressure. Lifting the blade at the end of a stroke, rather than dragging it back, prevents the return-stroke scratch that inexperienced cleaners create.

**Condition documentation before starting**: Professional companies that take their work seriously document the condition of glass before cleaning begins — particularly on post-construction, commercial, or high-value residential jobs. If there are existing scratches, chips, or surface damage on arrival, these are noted so there's no ambiguity about causation if a question arises after the clean.
bullets: Pre-rinse: every exterior pane before any tool contact — no exceptions
Tempered glass identification: check for manufacturer stamp, treat conservatively if unclear
Fresh razor blades: replaced each job, correct angle throughout, lifted on return stroke
Pre-clean documentation: photograph existing damage before starting on any risk job

sections.h2: Fabricating Debris and Pre-Existing Scratches: The Liability Question
sections.body:
Two phenomena create situations where scratches appear during or after a window clean but are not caused by the cleaning process. Understanding them is important for both homeowners and cleaning companies.

**Fabricating debris in tempered glass**: During the tempering process (rapid cooling of float glass), microscopic glass particles can become embedded in the glass surface. These are called fabricating debris and are invisible to the naked eye and untouchable by hand. When a cleaning tool passes over them, they scratch the glass from within — damage that looks identical to a squeegee scratch but was caused by the glass itself. This is a manufacturing quality defect that affects a percentage of tempered glass, and the scratches typically appear during the first professional clean after installation.

In BC, a Glass Association of North America (GANA) bulletin addresses this issue specifically: professional cleaners are not liable for scratches caused by fabricating debris, provided they used appropriate technique and tools for the glass type. However, this defense requires that the cleaner can demonstrate appropriate technique — which is why pre-rinse protocols, tool documentation, and pre-clean glass assessment matter.

**Pre-existing scratches revealed by cleaning**: Dirty glass hides surface scratches. Once the glass is clean and light hits it at a low angle, scratches that existed before the cleaning become clearly visible. Homeowners sometimes attribute these newly visible scratches to the cleaning. Professional cleaners who document pre-existing damage before starting avoid this ambiguity entirely.

The practical recommendation for homeowners: if you suspect scratches appeared during a professional clean, ask the company to walk through their documentation of pre-clean condition. A company that photographs pre-clean glass can resolve the question definitively. A company that has no documentation leaves the question unresolvable.
sections.body2: Pre-clean glass documentation is a professional standard that protects both the homeowner and the cleaner. Its absence is a flag that a company's quality systems may not be robust.

sections.h2: What to Do If Scratches Appear After a Professional Clean
sections.body:
If you notice scratches after a professional window cleaning visit that weren't visible before, here's the right process.

**Step 1: Assess the scratch characteristics.** Where are the scratches located? Do they follow the pattern of squeegee strokes (directional, parallel lines)? Are they concentrated in an area where a blade would have been used (paint overspray, adhesive)? Are they on tempered glass (sliding doors, low windows)? The pattern helps determine whether they're cleaning-related or pre-existing/fabricating debris.

**Step 2: Contact the company promptly.** Don't wait weeks. Most professional companies have a window (the actual time window, in this case) for addressing post-clean concerns. Contact within 24-48 hours of noticing the issue, while the visit is fresh in everyone's memory and the clean hasn't been followed by other events.

**Step 3: Describe specifically, don't accuse.** 'I noticed scratches on the lower left pane of the back bedroom window after your visit — they weren't visible before. Can you come have a look?' is more productive than 'Your cleaner scratched my windows.' The goal is resolution, and most professional companies want to resolve legitimate concerns.

**Step 4: Understand the resolution options.** Minor surface scratches on annealed glass can sometimes be reduced (not eliminated) with glass polishing compounds. Significant scratches typically require glass replacement. Whether this is at the company's cost depends on whether the scratching was caused by the cleaning process — which is where pre-clean documentation and technique evidence become relevant.

Wagon Windows documents pre-clean condition on post-construction and high-risk jobs and uses appropriate tools for each glass type. We stand behind our work and address any legitimate post-clean concerns directly.

Contact within 24-48 hours while the visit is fresh
Describe specifically, request an assessment visit — don't demand before understanding cause
Resolution depends on causation: company's fault = company pays; pre-existing = different conversation

faqs.q: Is tempered glass more likely to get scratched during window cleaning?
faqs.a: Tempered glass has a specific vulnerability to fabricating debris — microscopic glass particles embedded during manufacturing that create scratches when a cleaning tool passes over them. This is not caused by the cleaner but appears during cleaning, which creates confusion. Standard annealed glass can also be scratched if abrasive grit is present, but it doesn't have the fabricating debris issue. Both glass types need proper pre-rinse protocol.

faqs.q: Can a window cleaner use a razor blade without scratching glass?
faqs.a: Yes — when done correctly. A fresh, sharp blade at the correct angle (under 30 degrees to the glass surface), with sufficient cleaning solution as lubrication, on appropriate glass types, removes paint and adhesive without scratching. The errors that cause scratching are dull blades, incorrect angle, insufficient lubrication, and using blades on tempered glass without the proper precautions.

faqs.q: Who is responsible if a window gets scratched during professional cleaning?
faqs.a: If the scratch was caused by improper technique, inappropriate tools, or failure to pre-rinse — the company is responsible. If the scratch is from fabricating debris in tempered glass (a manufacturing defect revealed by cleaning) or from pre-existing damage that wasn't documented — responsibility is less clear. Pre-clean documentation by the company is the clearest way to establish what existed before the visit.

faqs.q: Can scratched glass be repaired, or does it need replacement?
faqs.a: Minor surface scratches on annealed glass can sometimes be improved (not fully eliminated) with professional glass polishing compounds that reduce scratch visibility through controlled abrasion of the surrounding surface. Deeper scratches and any scratches on tempered glass typically cannot be polished effectively and require glass replacement. A glass restoration company can assess whether polishing is viable before committing to replacement.
